title: Common-Cell Convergence + catalytic composition on real digits
interpretation: EXT08 (Common-Cell Convergence): four agents with MUTUALLY DISJOINT pixel representations (disjoint=True) nevertheless map inputs to the same label far above chance: mean pairwise agreement 0.51 vs chance 0.10; all four agree on 29% of inputs and are correct on 100% of those. This is a real instance of the theorem: coordination on the outcome cell (label) without any shared representation. EXT09 (catalytic composition): majority-vote error 0.160 is BELOW the best single agent 0.264 -- composing independent agents lowers the floor, as claimed. BUT the multiplicative 'independence' floor 0.0103 is far below the measured ensemble error: the agents are NOT independent (mean pairwise error-correlation 0.15>0, they share the task and fail on the same hard digits). HONEST CONCLUSION: the qualitative claims hold on real data (disjoint-rep convergence; ensemble beats best member), but the EXACT multiplicative composition law requires independence that real agents violate -- the paper must state the law as an independence-limit bound, not an equality.
